title: Lecturer Quiz: Positional Encoding & Transformers - Multiple Choice

questions.question:
Encoder models (like BERT) are trained using [ Select ] , where the model sees both left and right context. This allows them to learn bidirectional representations. They aren't [ Select ] because they can't [ Select ] . Decoder models (like GPT) are trained with [ Select ] , predicting the next token from past context only. These models are great for text generation, yet have less accurate latent representations since [ Select ] . Encoder-decoder models (like T5 or BART) combine both ideas: the encoder reads the full input sequence, while the decoder generates the output token-by-token, [ Select ] . This setup makes them ideal for [ Select ] .

questions.question:
Looking at the Positional Embedding formula, fill in the blanks: PE(pos,2i) =sin( pos 100002i/dmodel ) PE(pos,2i+1) =cos( pos 100002i/dmodel ) It important that we compute each positional embedding value using 2i and 2i + 1 because [ Select ] For lower embedding dimensions i the values of the respective entry in the positional embedding will change [ Select ] , which means that the positional embedding can capture variations at [ Select ] . For higher dimensions instead, the rate of change will be [ Select ] , meaning that these dimensions capture changes that happen across [ Select ] of contexts.

Select the correct choice for blank #1:

options.A: it keeps all frequencies constant across dimensions
options.B: the offset of 1 unit changes the frequencies that the embeddings capture
options.C: it allows each position to be represented by unique combinations of periodic functions
options.D: it ensures that positional embeddings are all identical

In the Transformer architecture, the encoder–decoder attention allows the decoder to attend to the encoder’s outputs. During this step: The queries come from the [ Select ] , while the keys and values come from the [ Select ] . This means that the decoder can focus on relevant parts of the encoder’s output sequence while generating each token. Formally, if h1,…,hn are the encoder outputs and z1,…,zn​ are the decoder inputs, then: ki=Khi,vi=Vhi,qi=Qzi Here, ki and vi are derived from the encoder’s [ Select ] , while qicomes from the decoder’s [ Select ] .

Understanding Sinusoidal Positional Encoding Transformers do not have any built-in notion of sequence order. To help the model understand the position of tokens in a sequence, we add a positional encoding to the input embeddings. Below is a sample implementation: import torch import math def positional_encoding(seq_len, d_model): pe = torch.zeros(seq_len, d_model) position = torch.arange(0, seq_len, dtype=torch.float).unsqueeze(1) div_term = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)) pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(position * div_term) pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(position * div_term) return pe Fill in the blanks to test your understanding: The goal of positional encoding is to inject information about [ Select ] into the model, which it otherwise lacks due to its architecture. The encoding uses [ Select ] applied to a scaled version of the token position to generate unique patterns per position. The encoding is designed such that it can generalize to [ Select ] sequences beyond those seen during training, due to its use of continuous functions. Instead of learned embeddings, this version uses a [ Select ] method based on math, which does not add trainable parameters. The term div_term scales the position using [ Select ] , which control how frequencies are distributed across dimensions.

Select the correct choice for blank #1:

You are building an NLP system that must both classify political statements by stance (agree/disagree/neutral) and generate short summaries of each statement. You are considering three possible model types: Encoder-only, Decoder-only, and Encoder–Decoder architectures. For each case below, choose the correct description of how the model would process the task. If you use an Encoder-only model (e.g., BERT): [ Select ] If you use a Decoder-only model (e.g., GPT): [ Select ] If you use an Encoder–Decoder model (e.g., T5 or BART): [ Select ] For the stance classification part of your task, the most suitable architecture is: [ Select ] For the summarization part of your task, the best-suited architecture is: [ Select ]

Select the correct choice for blank #1:

options.A: You input one token at a time, predicting the next token sequentially, and use the average of all outputs for classification.
options.B: You input the whole text, the model reads it bidirectionally, and you use the [CLS] token representation (or pooled output) for classification.
options.C: You mask some tokens and reconstruct them entirely, using the reconstruction loss directly for classification.
options.D: You feed both input and output sentences separately, cross-attending them to produce contextualized decoder vectors for classification.

questions.question:
You are building an NLP system that must both classify political statements by stance (agree/disagree/neutral) and generate short summaries of each statement. You are considering three possible model types: Encoder-only, Decoder-only, and Encoder–Decoder architectures. For each case below, choose the correct description of how the model would process the task. If you use an Encoder-only model (e.g., BERT): [ Select ] If you use a Decoder-only model (e.g., GPT): [ Select ] If you use an Encoder–Decoder model (e.g., T5 or BART): [ Select ] For the stance classification part of your task, the most suitable architecture is: [ Select ] For the summarization part of your task, the best-suited architecture is: [ Select ]

Select the correct choice for blank #2:

options.A: You first encode the text with an encoder network and then generate the summary using a decoder.
options.B: You provide the full input and output sentences together, training bidirectional attention across all tokens.
options.C: You train it to predict masked tokens using both left and right context, learning bidirectional dependencies rather than generating text sequentially.
options.D: You can feed the model with the sentence and task prompt (e.g., “Summarize: ...”) and have it generate the summary directly, token by token.

questions.question:
You are building an NLP system that must both classify political statements by stance (agree/disagree/neutral) and generate short summaries of each statement. You are considering three possible model types: Encoder-only, Decoder-only, and Encoder–Decoder architectures. For each case below, choose the correct description of how the model would process the task. If you use an Encoder-only model (e.g., BERT): [ Select ] If you use a Decoder-only model (e.g., GPT): [ Select ] If you use an Encoder–Decoder model (e.g., T5 or BART): [ Select ] For the stance classification part of your task, the most suitable architecture is: [ Select ] For the summarization part of your task, the best-suited architecture is: [ Select ]

Select the correct choice for blank #3:

options.A: You employ only the encoder component to transform the input into hidden representations and map these directly to class logits or token probabilities, skipping the generative decoding phase.
options.B: You input a task prompt and the full text, and the model predicts masked tokens directly from the encoder representations without using a decoding stage, focusing only on understanding the input.
options.C: You rely on the decoder alone to process both input and output sequences under causal masking, then fine-tune the decoder for the classification task
options.D: You first encode the entire input sequence bidirectionally, then use a decoder that attends to the encoder’s output while generating the summary token by token.

questions.question:
Complete the sentences with the correct continuations about this code: class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d_model: int, dropout: float = 0.1, max_len: int = 5000): super().__init__() self.dropout = nn.Dropout(p=dropout) position = torch.arange(max_len).XX freq = (10000.0 ** (-2 * torch.arange(0, d_model, 2) / d_model)).unsqueeze(0) pe = torch.zeros(max_len, 1, d_model) pe[:, 0, 0::2] = torch.XX(position * freq) pe[:, 0, 1::2] = torch.cos(position * freq) self.register_buffer('pe', pe) def forward(self, x): """ Arguments: x: Tensor, shape [seq_len, batch_size, embedding_dim] """ x = x + self.pe[:x.size(0)] return self.dropout(x) 1) Complete the line: position = torch.arange(max_len). [ Select ] This operation reshapes the position indices so they can be broadcast correctly with the frequency tensor. 2) Complete the sinusoidal assignment: pe[:, 0, 0::2] = torch. [ Select ] (position * freq) Even embedding dimensions use one trigonometric function, while odd dimensions use another. 3) Complete the explanation: return self.dropout(x) Dropout is applied after adding positional encodings to [ Select ] .

Select the correct choice for blank #1:
